problem_summary: 2020 MCM Problem A (continuous): model how the warming North Atlantic forces Atlantic herring and Atlantic mackerel to migrate north over the next 50 years, quantify the economic impact on small Scottish fishing companies (those with sub-12 m trawlers and a ~51 km harvesting radius), and recommend whether/when/where they should relocate. Team 2017785 (Outstanding) decomposed it into three coupled sub-models: (1) a per-coordinate linear regression of sea-surface temperature (SST) vs. time; (2) a stochastic agent-based NetLogo simulation where fish schools migrate on a daily-updated temperature field by gradient descent toward their optimal temperature; (3) a fishing-company revenue/"disappearance-time" model overlaid on the migration sim, swept across 87 coastal port locations. They then ran a 95%-CI best/worst-case sensitivity analysis and tested three concrete mitigation strategies (relocate port, switch to haddock, add on-board refrigeration).
models_used: Per-coordinate linear regression of SST vs. time: T(i,j) = A*(days since 1982) + B, fit independently in Python for every cell of an 80x64 grid (0.25 deg resolution, ~27.75 km cells) over NOAA OISST 1982-present; residual plots checked for structure to justify linearity. Yields a spatially-varying warming field (A ranges 0.00002-0.00015 C/day) rather than a single global average.

Continuous gradient-descent formulation of fish movement: define D_t = |T_s(x,y) - F_ot| (distance from optimal temp); fish move along -grad(D_t), with the path as a vector-valued function R(t) where R'(t) = -grad(D_t), integrated to get x(t), y(t). This is the continuous-math 'spine' that justifies the discrete simulation.

Agent-based model (NetLogo): each agent = one school (~20,000 fish). Discrete analog of the gradient: a school checks its 8 neighboring (Moore) patches and steps toward the one closest to its optimal temperature. Per-species behavioral thresholds — optimal / staying / tolerable temperature (Herring 4.6/9/12 C, Mackerel 5/12/25 C). Rules: don't move if below staying temp; 'escape mode' (random walk <=10 days) to break out of local minima; school dies after 7 days above tolerable temp. Temperature field updated every tick (1 tick = 1 day) from the regression.
Initial spatial distribution model: 70% of schools seeded from real ICES statistical-rectangle catch-density data (2003-2006 means), 30% randomly distributed to cover unrecorded fish.

Fishing-company revenue model layered on the sim: port sends 10 vessels/day, each visits every school within the 51 km radius, catches a random 3-7% catch proportion per school until vessel capacity (1750 mackerel / 2800 herring) is hit; revenue = fish caught * price-per-fish ($0.50 mackerel). 'Disappearance time' = last day any school is within radius.

Unit-conversion / first-principles parameter estimation (dimensional analysis): population counts, schools, vessel capacity, catch proportion, and price-per-fish all derived from Scottish Government landings tonnage and average fish mass rather than guessed.

Sensitivity analysis via NOAA per-measurement standard deviations: re-fit regressions after adding/subtracting 1.96*std.dev (95% CI) to get best/worst warming scenarios, then re-ran the full ABM to bound disappearance times.
key_techniques: Spatially-resolved regression instead of one global warming number — fitting A,B per grid cell is the single most-praised move; it lets eastern England warm ~2 C while waters east of Iceland barely change, producing realistic directional migration.

Bridging continuous PDE-style math to a discrete agent simulation: they derive -grad(D_t) = R'(t) on paper, then implement the equivalent 8-neighbor greedy step in NetLogo. This gives the paper both analytic rigor (it reads as a continuous problem) and a runnable, visualizable model.

Per-species threshold parameterization (optimal/staying/tolerable) sourced to literature — a clean mechanism that makes herring vs. mackerel behave differently (herring flee fast, mackerel tolerate up to 25 C and linger), which the results then explain.
Local-minimum 'escape mode' random walk — an honest fix for a known greedy-gradient failure mode, showing the team anticipated a simulation artifact.

Full parameter sweep: ran 3 repeat sims at each of 87 coastline ports and rendered the output as geographic heat-maps of disappearance time and revenue — converts a stochastic model into an actionable map a fisherman can read.

Sensitivity bounds tied to actual data uncertainty (NOAA std.dev, 1.96 sigma) rather than arbitrary +/-10% perturbation — and they surfaced a counterintuitive result (faster warming HELPS some east-coast mackerel ports) and explained the mechanism instead of hiding it.

Each proposed strategy is re-run through the same model to produce numbers (e.g., on-board refrigeration: +43% revenue in the north to +1700% in the south), so recommendations are quantified, not asserted.
why_it_won: "It nails the MCM judging trifecta: a clever-but-defensible modeling choice, end-to-end execution, and decision-relevant output. The spatially-varying regression is genuinely insightful and the team explicitly calls out why it beats a global-average warming rate. The continuous-to-discrete derivation gives mathematical credibility while the ABM gives vivid, year-by-year visualizations and natural handling of stochasticity. Crucially, every parameter is built from real, cited Scottish/NOAA/ICES data via transparent dimensional analysis — judges can trace any number back to a source, which reads as trustworthy rather than hand-wavy. The work answers the actual client question (small fishing companies) concretely: named best ports (Shetland, Orkney, Aberdeenshire), dated migration-out timelines (herring ~2052, mackerel ~2064), dollar revenues per location, and three costed strategies each validated by re-running the model. The sensitivity analysis is rigorous AND yields a surprising, well-explained finding, signaling deep understanding. Finally the presentation — a mock newspaper front-page executive summary plus full appendices (regression coefficients, per-coordinate revenue tables, the complete NetLogo source) — shows both communication polish and reproducibility."
section_highlights: "Front matter: a one-page mock newspaper ('A Fishy Situation', VOL.I No.1) that delivers the executive summary as a layperson-readable story with the 4 strategies up top — memorable framing judges remember. Section 1.2 Problem Analysis ends with a bulleted map of each sub-question to the exact section answering it (a roadmap judges love). Section 2.2: ~12 explicit assumption/justification pairs, each tied to a citation. Section 2.3 (temperature): clear equation, A/B heat-maps, and a residual-plot argument for linearity. Section 2.4.4 vs 2.4.5: the standout pairing — first the continuous gradient/vector-calculus derivation, then 'our NetLogo model follows an equivalent logic but on a discrete temperature surface,' explicitly bridging theory and implementation. Section 3.3 + Fig 8: 87-location heat-maps of disappearance time and revenue. Section 3.3.1: a genuine sensitivity table (Table 2) with the counterintuitive mackerel result explained mechanistically. Section 4: three strategies each with its own re-run numbers (Table 3 refrigeration % increases). Section 6 Strengths/Weaknesses is candid and specific. Appendices A-D give regression coefficients, full revenue tables, and the entire NetLogo source code."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Single-driver migration: movement depends ONLY on temperature — no predation, food, salinity, fishing pressure, or density dependence (schools explicitly don't interact). The team's 'temperature correlates with food' hand-wave is plausible but unvalidated.

Linear SST extrapolation 50 years out: a straight line fit to 1982-present is extrapolated to 2070, ignoring acceleration/non-linearity of warming and any climate feedbacks — fragile at the far horizon.

Greedy 8-neighbor step is a coarse, grid-anisotropic approximation of true gradient descent; 'escape mode' patches local minima but movement is still biased toward the 8 lattice directions and step calibration to real swim speed is weak.

Constant population assumption: herring/mackerel stocks held fixed for 50 years (births offset deaths + quotas), so the model can't capture stock collapse, which is a real risk under warming and migration.
Disappearance-time averages are censored at 2070 (the paper admits these 'do not produce true averages'), biasing long-lived locations.
Revenue is acknowledged as an overestimate — EEZ/quota loss to Iceland/Greenland is discussed qualitatively in Section 5 but never folded into the dollar figures.

Several parameters come from low-authority or mismatched sources (Wikipedia, a cast-net National Geographic page for net weight, PETA for bycatch) and are mixed across incompatible contexts (the team flags this 'waterfall effect' themselves).
No seasonality: model is explicitly invalid for sub-1-year or single-day predictions; the 'seasonal patterns naturally cancel' argument is asserted, not demonstrated.
Only 3 repeat runs per location for a stochastic model — thin Monte Carlo coverage for the revenue/disappearance-time variance being reported.
reusable_patterns: Per-cell regression over a spatial grid: when a driver varies geographically, fit an independent simple model at each (lat,lon) cell instead of one global parameter — cheap, data-grounded, and produces realistic spatial heterogeneity that impresses judges.

Continuous-then-discrete pattern: derive the governing behavior in clean continuous math (gradient/vector calculus, ODE path), then state that the simulation implements 'an equivalent logic on a discrete surface.' Buys analytic credibility plus a runnable, visualizable model.

Agent-based modeling as the default for 'lots of interacting stochastic entities in a changing field' problems (migration, crowds, epidemics, traffic) — and explicitly add an escape/random-perturbation rule to handle greedy local minima.
Behavioral threshold ladders (optimal/staying/tolerable) sourced per entity type to create emergent qualitative differences the results section can then interpret.
Build EVERY parameter from cited primary data via visible dimensional analysis (tonnage * grams/ton * 1/avg-mass = count). Makes the model auditable and is far more convincing than assumed constants.
Sweep the decision variable across all real candidate locations and render results as geographic heat-maps of the two metrics the client cares about (here: time-until-loss and revenue).
Tie sensitivity perturbations to the data's own reported uncertainty (here NOAA std.dev, 1.96 sigma for 95% CI) and proactively explain any counterintuitive output rather than burying it.
Validate each recommended strategy by re-running the same model under that strategy to produce a concrete delta (% revenue change), so advice is quantified.

Communication scaffolding: a roadmap that maps sub-questions to sections, paired assumption/justification tables, a layperson executive summary (their newspaper gimmick), and appendices containing raw coefficient tables AND the full simulation source for reproducibility.
